ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic toast entry (FOODON_03315091). No specific entry exists for multigrain kaya thick toast; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and similar products. No specific code exists for kaya toast; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for multigrain kaya thick toast as a specific food concept.

serving.description: 1 serving of multigrain kaya thick toast (2 slices with kaya spread)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of two thick slices of multigrain toast spread generously with kaya, weighing approximately 100 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Kaya Toast (Hainanese-style) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy multigrain bread adapted with kaya spread component
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for kaya toast (standard white bread variant). Values for multigrain kaya thick toast are adapted by substituting multigrain bread nutritional profile (USDA SR Legacy item 18069, whole-wheat bread) for the white bread base, and applying kaya spread contribution (~15 g per 100 g serving) from HPB Foodo kaya spread entry. Energy and macros are composite estimates. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Bread Products — Multigrain and wholemeal bread analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for multigrain kaya thick toast. GI of 65 is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for multigrain bread (GI range 48–69, mean ~65) combined with the effect of kaya spread (high sugar content raises effective GI slightly above plain multigrain bread). Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (65 × 49.5) / 100 = 32.2.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size as the sodium content from bread and kaya spread contributes meaningfully to daily sodium targets; one serving provides approximately 310 mg sodium, roughly 13% of the 2,400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~65) and high sugar content from kaya spread (~14.2 g sugar per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume multigrain kaya thick toast in moderation; the medium GI of approximately 65 and the high sugar load from kaya spread can cause a significant postprandial glucose rise, and pairing with a protein source such as a soft-boiled egg may help blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from kaya spread (coconut milk base, ~3.5 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, the saturated fat contributed by the coconut-milk-based kaya spread should be accounted for within daily saturated fat limits; choosing a smaller portion or a reduced-sugar kaya variant is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Multigrain Kaya Thick Toast is a popular Singaporean breakfast and snack item consisting of two thick slices of toasted multigrain bread generously spread with kaya, a traditional Hainanese coconut jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan leaf. The multigrain bread base provides a denser, nuttier texture and slightly higher fibre content compared to the classic white-bread version, while the kaya spread imparts a sweet, fragrant, and creamy flavour with a distinctive pandan aroma. The toast is typically served warm and pairs traditionally with soft-boiled eggs seasoned with soy sauce and white pepper, and a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea). Nutritionally, one 100 g serving delivers approximately 298 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 7.8 g fat (of which 3.5 g is saturated from the coconut-milk kaya), and 49.5 g carbohydrate with 3.8 g dietary fibre. The medium glycemic index (~65) and high sugar content (~14.2 g per 100 g) from the kaya spread make portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) is relevant for those managing hypertension. The multigrain base contributes B vitamins, iron, and dietary fibre relative to white-bread variants. This dish is a cultural staple of Singaporean kopitiam (coffee shop) culture and is widely available at chains such as Toast Box, Ya Kun Kaya Toast, and Killiney Kopitiam.
common_user_queries: How many calories in multigrain kaya toast?
Is kaya toast high in sugar?
Toast Box multigrain kaya toast calories
Multigrain vs white bread kaya toast

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists specifically for multigrain kaya thick toast. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo kaya toast (standard) and USDA SR Legacy multigrain/whole-wheat bread data. The adaptation introduces moderate uncertainty in exact macro values, particularly fat and sugar from the kaya spread proportion.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from HPB Foodo kaya toast entry and USDA SR Legacy whole-wheat bread (item 18069). Kaya spread contribution estimated at approximately 15 g per 100 g of finished product. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace in coconut-based kaya and multigrain bread per USDA SR Legacy; confirmed zero by formulation). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero in toasted bread and coconut-egg kaya; no vitamin C-containing ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries for this food category. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this food. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database multigrain bread entries; no direct GI measurement for kaya thick toast was found. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 49.5) / 100 = 32.2 per 100 g serving. Halal status assumed based on standard kaya ingredients (no lard); kosher status not confirmed. Allergen list includes coconut as a tree nut per Singapore Food Agency allergen labelling guidance.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with full consistency check performed.
Adapted nutrient values from HPB Foodo kaya toast and USDA SR Legacy multigrain bread; documented adaptation basis in nutrient_source.
Set GI to 65 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database multigrain bread entries); documented estimation basis in gi_source.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-not-reported justification in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg) with confirmed zero / naturally zero justification in notes.
Applied correct ontology match_type values (closest_parent, none) only; no invented codes.
Included all three required health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Ensured natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
